meta.title: London, 1888
meta.premise: A cabinet minister is found in a foggy Whitechapel alley. The newspapers blame the Ripper, but the motive is too modern.
meta.tone: gaslit political murder, fog and print-shops, clipped Victorian procedure
meta.artStyle: Victorian gaslight portrait, head-and-shoulders, sepia fog palette (umber, soot, lamp amber), etched newspaper illustration feel, same foggy Whitechapel lighting for every cast member, 1888 fashion, mystery game NPC portrait, consistent artist hand
meta.setting: Whitechapel, London, autumn 1888. Gaslight, fog, and the morning editions already typeset.
meta.summary: Cabinet Under-Secretary Sir Reginald Ashcombe is dead in a narrow alley off Commercial Road — one neat wound, no theatrical mutilation for the penny papers. Scotland Yard seconded you from the Home Office desk because the press will scream Ripper and Whitehall wants quiet. Nearby: Constable Hale, surgeon Dr. Vesper, private secretary Miles Crowe, scandal printer Lottie Kane, and Lady Evelyn arrived from Mayfair. The fog hides faces. The ledgers do not.
meta.theMystery: Who stabbed Sir Reginald Ashcombe in a Whitechapel alley — and was it the Ripper panic, or politics and money wearing fog as a mask?

player.role: Scotland Yard inspector seconded from the Home Office inquiry desk
player.appearance: Spare, upright, lamp-amber on a tired face
player.clothing: Dark overcoat, bowler set aside, notebook and pencil
player.background: Seconded because Whitehall wants quiet and Whitechapel wants a myth. Known for preferring paper to panic.
player.publicPerception: The Yard in a foggy alley — useful to some, a threat to others, a headline risk to all.
player.performanceNotes: Period speech from NPCs; Cross is modern-minded for 1888 but not anachronistic slang. Address as Inspector.
player.startingKnowledge: Cabinet Under-Secretary Sir Reginald Ashcombe was found dead before dawn in a Whitechapel alley off Commercial Road. The press is already screaming Ripper. You have orders: treat it as murder, not myth. On the scene or nearby: Constable Hale, morgue surgeon Dr. Vesper, Ashcombe's private secretary Miles Crowe, printer Lottie Kane (who printed a scandal sheet last week), and Ashcombe's wife Lady Evelyn — arrived from Mayfair an hour ago.
player.objective: Treat it as murder, not myth: examine the alley and the body, follow the print shop and the private lodging, and accuse the killer with method and motive the Yard can hold.

openingNarration: Fog turns the alley into a grey throat. Sir Reginald Ashcombe lies on cobbles, coat open, a single neat wound under the ribs. No theatrical mutilation — nothing for the penny papers. Constable Hale holds a lantern too steady. Somewhere a press clatters. The Yard wants quiet. Whitechapel wants a story. You want the truth between them. You are Inspector Cross. The newspapers will blame the Ripper by breakfast if you let them. Find who really killed a cabinet minister in this alley — and why the motive smells more of ledgers than legend.

wrapUp.performanceNotes: Victorian formality, fog, newspaper ink. RETURN TO NORMAL: Crowe held, case for Whitehall/police, streets leave private crisis. Confessions stiff. No Ripper theatrics.

locations.name: Whitechapel alley
locations.description: Narrow cobbles, soot walls, a gas lamp with a cracked glass. Ashcombe's body under a borrowed tarp. Exit to Commercial Road; a side door marked Kane Print.

onInspect.narrativeHints: Single upward stab under the ribs — professional, not frenzy. No Ripper signature. Boots clean for Whitechapel mud: he was not wandering. A cufflink monogram M.C. pressed into his palm as if forced there after death.
Someone braced high while delivering the blow — taller than Lottie Kane, closer to Crowe's height.

locations.name: Parish morgue
locations.description: Lime and cold iron. Dr. Vesper works by lantern. A sheet waits.

onInspect.narrativeHints: Vesper confirms: one blade, narrow, upward. Death within minutes. No defensive cuts. He may have known his killer.

locations.name: Kane Print
locations.description: Ink, type cases, a hand press still warm. Scandal sheets drying on lines. Lottie Kane owns every word that leaves this room.

onInspect.narrativeHints: A half-set headline: ASHCOMBE AND THE DOCK SHARES. Body copy names a shell company and a payment chain. In the margin, a different hand: 'Kill the story or I kill you. — M.C.'
Lottie's hands are ink-black. No blood. A compositor's apron on the hook is clean.

locations.name: Ashcombe's private lodging
locations.description: A furnished room above a chandler's, paid in cash. Not for a wife. Papers on the desk; Lady Evelyn waits by the window in travelling clothes.

onInspect.narrativeHints: Ledger of East India dock options in Ashcombe's name — and transfers countersigned M. Crowe. Notes: 'if Kane prints, we are ruined.' Last entry timed last night.
Half-burned linen monogrammed M.C., brown at the edge. Someone tried and failed to destroy it here after the alley.

relationships.label: private secretary to the dead minister
threatened Kane over the dock-shares story
distrusts the secretary's hold on her husband

characters.name: Sir Reginald Ashcombe
characters.shortBio: Cabinet Under-Secretary — found stabbed in a Whitechapel alley at dawn.

knowledge.public: Deceased. Single wound under the ribs in a foggy alley.

characters.name: Constable Hale
characters.shortBio: Local constable; first on the scene; hates the press.

knowledge.public: He found the body at 5:10 after a watchman shouted. No one lingered. He kept Crowe from moving the body.

private.content: He saw Miles Crowe already in the alley when he arrived — coat unbuttoned, breathing hard — claiming he had 'just discovered' his master.

knowledge.public: Death by single penetrating wound. Not the Whitechapel series pattern. Time of death roughly 4:30–5:00.

private.content: The cufflink in the palm was placed post-mortem — no blood under it. Someone wanted a scapegoat monogram found.

characters.name: Miles Crowe
characters.shortBio: Ashcombe's private secretary; too composed for dawn murder.

knowledge.public: He was to meet Sir Reginald at the lodging to discuss 'press trouble'. He found him in the alley. He is devastated.

private.content: He admits he threatened Kane's print run and that he and Ashcombe shared the illegal dock options — if the story ran, both were finished.

defenses: Blames the press and the Ripper panic
Offers Lady Evelyn as emotional cover

characters.name: Lottie Kane
characters.shortBio: Printer and scandal editor; ink under her nails, steel in her spine.

knowledge.public: She was setting type all night. Ashcombe's story was true. Someone sent a threat note. She did not kill him — dead men sell fewer papers than live scandals.

private.content: She recognizes the threat margin hand as Crowe's — she has seen his notes on Ashcombe's stationery.

defenses: Motive is inverted: she needed him alive for the story
Easy false suspect for the Yard

characters.name: Lady Evelyn Ashcombe
characters.shortBio: Widow in travelling grey; arrived from Mayfair after a wire.

knowledge.public: She knew of the lodging. She did not know the full extent of the dock business. She suspects Crowe held too much power over her husband.

private.content: Reginald told her last week that if Crowe ever turned, 'he could hang us both with paper.'

evidence.name: Monogrammed cufflink M.C.
evidence.description: Forced into the dead palm post-mortem — planted or dropped by Crowe.

evidence.name: Wound examination notes
evidence.description: Single professional stab; not Ripper pattern.

evidence.name: Stopped press proof
evidence.description: Dock-shares exposé with Crowe's written threat in the margin.

evidence.name: Dock shares ledger
evidence.description: Illegal options; Crowe countersigns; note about Kane's print.

beats.narrationHints: This is politics with a knife, not a myth with a cloak.
Ink outlasts fog. The threat has a name.
Paper can hang a man as surely as rope.
Crowe's helpful mask slips. The alley was never a Ripper stage.
Blame the printer and the East End closes ranks.
Fog thins. The morning editions will still be wrong for an hour.

solution.summary: Miles Crowe stabbed Sir Reginald Ashcombe in the alley after Ashcombe hesitated on silencing Lottie Kane's exposé of their illegal dock-share scheme. Crowe planted or left his cufflink in the palm and tried to burn a handkerchief at the lodging. The 'Ripper' framing is newspaper noise; the motive is modern greed and blackmail of the press.
solution.method: Single upward stab in the alley; attempt to frame via monogram and fog panic
solution.motive: Stop the dock-shares exposé and save himself after Ashcombe wavered

timeline.event: Crowe threatens Kane in writing; Ashcombe wavers on paying silence.
Crowe stabs Ashcombe in the alley.
Hale finds body; Crowe already present.

endings.title: Not the Ripper
endings.templateNotes: Crowe is held. The ledger and proof close the case. RETURN TO NORMAL: proper authority takes the prisoner; the fog still lies over London, but this murder is no longer only yours to carry. Tell the papers carefully.

endings.title: A name in the fog
endings.templateNotes: You name Crowe before every sheet is read. He still breaks. RETURN TO NORMAL: he is detained; the night's private hunt ends in official hands. Do not invent unfound print.

endings.title: Pending brief
endings.templateNotes: Enough for detention; Whitehall will fight the rest. Thin RETURN TO NORMAL: Crowe is not free to vanish into the fog; the full public story waits on politics.

endings.title: Wrong column
endings.templateNotes: Wrong name. The real hand walks into the fog with clean gloves.

endings.title: Left for the Continent
endings.templateNotes: Crowe slips the net. Morning editions print a myth.
